result: I keep returning to that dim hour just before sunrise when whatever wall separates the possible from the impossible turns porous. It’s not poetic; it’s biological—core temperature low, prefrontal cortex drowsy, limbic system left briefly in charge. Yet something feels sacred in those minutes, as though my skepticism is still asleep while the rest of me tiptoes around universe-sized questions without tripping over answers. I once used that hush to rehearse courage: picturing emails unsent, train platforms never stepped onto, forgivenesses never offered. Sometimes the exercise felt absurd, like praying to insomnia, yet choices I later made in full daylight seemed pre-warmed by those half-dreamed rehearsals—proof, maybe, that imagination is simply memory traveling backward. Yesterday the cat trotted in carrying a still-fluttering moth, released it under the bed, and watched gravity glue the insect to dust. That small tragedy felt heavier than the wars scrolling down my feed. I found myself kneeling, coaxing the dingy wing with a slip of paper, carrying it to the windowsill where sunrise could give it a second debut. It stayed, pulsing, until the first orange shard struck its scales into emerald—then flew. I don’t know if it survived the day: birds patrol these gutters like low satellites. Still, the moment keeps unfolding inside me, a portable dawn. Maybe every life is a series of miniature resurrections we rarely notice, private sunrises spilling across the kitchen floor while the kettle decides to whistle. We’re each other's impossible daybreaks, rehearsed—or refused—in silence.
